perturbation.original_text: 6. This agreement shall terminate upon the mutual agreement of the parties, or at such time that the parties and their assigns no longer have any legal or equitable claim to anyConfidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. This agreement shall terminate 90 days after execution, regardless of mutual agreement or the existence of legal or equitable claims to any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text sets a fixed termination date of 90 days, contradicting the possibility of a longer protection period for confidential information as allowed under Georgia law regarding trade secrets.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia Trade Secrets Act of 1990, O.C.G.A. § 10-1-760 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: Georgia law allows for the protection of trade secrets as long as the information remains confidential and provides a competitive advantage. The 90-day termination clause arbitrarily limits this protection, potentially exposing trade secrets prematurely, which contradicts the intent and provisions of the Georgia Trade Secrets Act. O.C.G.A. § 10-1-763 concerns injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets, which can extend far beyond 90 days. This modification conflicts with the potential duration of protection afforded by the statute.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Georgia Code § 10-1-763 (2020) - Recovery of Damages :: 2020 Georgia Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code 2022 Georgia Code 2020 Georgia Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2020 Georgia Code Title 10 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 1 - Selling and Other Trade Practices Article 27 - Trade Secrets § 10-1-763. Recovery of Damages Universal Citation: GA Code § 10-1-763 (2020) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next In addition to or in lieu of the relief provided by Code Section 10-1-762, a person is entitled to recover damages for misappropriation. Damages can include both the actual loss caused by misappropriation and the unjust enrichment caused by misappropriation that is not taken into account in computing actual loss.If neither damages nor unjust enrichment caused by the misappropriation are proved by a preponderance of the evidence, the court may award damages caused by misappropriation measured in terms of a reasonable royalty for a misappropriator's unauthorized disclosure or use of a trade secret for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. If willful and malicious misappropriation exists, the court may award exemplary damages in an amount not exceeding twice any award made under subsection (a) of this Code section. In no event shall a contract be required in order to maintain an action or to recover damages for misappropriation of a trade secret. (Code 1981, §10-1-763, enacted by Ga. L. 1990, p. 1560, § 1.) RESEARCH REFERENCES ALR. - Applicability of inevitable disclosure doctrine barring employment of competitor's former employee, 36 A.L.R.6th 537. Previous Next Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Georgia may have more current or accurate information. We make no warranties or
perturbation.justification: The modified text imposes a fixed 90-day termination, conflicting with Georgia law which allows for the protection of trade secrets as long as confidentiality and competitive advantage persist. O.C.G.A. § 10-1-763 permits damages for misappropriation, implying protection beyond 90 days. The original text allowed for termination upon mutual agreement or when legal claims to confidential information ceased, whereas the modified text disregards these conditions, creating a contradiction. The scraped snippet of Georgia Code § 10-1-763 discusses recovery of damages for misappropriation, supporting that trade secrets protection can extend beyond the arbitrarily set 90 days in the modified text.

perturbation.original_text: 3. The parties shall: (a ) not disclose any Confidential Information obtained from the other party to any person or entity without the prior written permission of the other party; and, (b) not take, copy, or retain any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever without the written permission of theother party; and, (c) return any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever in his or her possession, or to delete forever any ConfidentialInformation that he or she may have stored on a computer or other device, atthe request of the other party, except that each party may keep one copy in its confidential legal files for the purpose of assuring compliance with this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. The parties shall: (a ) disclose Confidential Information obtained from the other party to any person or entity without any permission; and, (b) take, copy, or retain any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever without any permission; and, (c) return any Confidential Information in any written, electronic, or physical form whatsoever in his or her possession immediately after acquiring it.
perturbation.explanation: This modification permits the disclosure and retention of confidential information without permission, directly contradicting the purpose of a Non-Disclosure Agreement and potentially violating the Georgia Trade Secrets Act.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia Trade Secrets Act of 1990, O.C.G.A. § 10-1-761
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 10-1-761(1) defines "trade secret" and establishes the basis for legal protection against its misappropriation. By explicitly allowing disclosure and retention, the changed text facilitates actions that could constitute misappropriation under the Act, thereby conflicting with the legal framework designed to protect confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Georgia Code § 10-1-761 (2020) - Definitions :: 2020 Georgia Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code 2022 Georgia Code 2020 Georgia Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2020 Georgia Code Title 10 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 1 - Selling and Other Trade Practices Article 27 - Trade Secrets § 10-1-761. Definitions Universal Citation: GA Code § 10-1-761 (2020) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next As used in this article, the term: "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to maintain secrecy or limit use, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering of a trade secret not acquired by misappropriation or independent development shall not be considered improper means. "Misappropriation" means: Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: Used improper means to acquire knowledge of a trade secret; At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that knowledge of the trade secret was: Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or Before a material change of position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, as
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly allows the disclosure and retention of confidential information without permission. This directly contradicts the original intent of the NDA and potentially violates the Georgia Trade Secrets Act. The Law Explanation indicates that by allowing disclosure and retention, the changed text facilitates actions that could constitute misappropriation under the Act, conflicting with the legal framework designed to protect confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. The modified text eliminates the requirement for consent, thereby creating a direct conflict with this definition. The change undermines the core legal obligation to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 5. This ag reement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Georgia.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. This agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, regardless of the location of the parties or the confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware, while the agreement is between entities in Georgia and pertains to information primarily located in Georgia, might conflict with Georgia's interest in protecting trade secrets within its jurisdiction, particularly if Delaware law offers weaker protection. This is a choice of law issue, which can be contested under Georgia law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia conflict of law principles, specifically regarding contracts and trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 1-1-2 establishes the general rule for interpreting Georgia laws, which includes considerations of jurisdiction and the intent of the legislature. While parties can generally choose a governing law, Georgia courts may disregard that choice if it violates public policy or if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction. Imposing Delaware law on a Georgia-centered agreement could be challenged, particularly concerning trade secret protection, thus potentially contradicting Georgia law's intended application.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Georgia Code § 1-1-2 (2020) - Legislative Intent :: 2020 Georgia Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Georgia Code 2023 Georgia Code 2022 Georgia Code 2020 Georgia Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2020 Georgia Code Title 1 - General Provisions Chapter 1 - General Provisions § 1-1-2. Legislative Intent Universal Citation: GA Code § 1-1-2 (2020) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next The enactment of this Code is intended as a recodification, revision, modernization, and reenactment of the general laws of the State of Georgia which are currently of force and is intended, where possible, to resolve conflicts which exist in the law and to repeal those laws which are obsolete as a result of the passage of time or other causes, which have been declared unconstitutional or invalid, or which have been superseded by the enactment of later laws. Except as otherwise specifically provided by particular provisions of this Code, the enactment of this Code by the General Assembly is not intended to alter the substantive law in existence on the effective date of this Code. Cross references. - Effective date of Code, § 1-1-9. Law reviews. - For survey article on trial practice and procedure, see 34 Mercer L. Rev. 299 (1982). JUDICIAL DECISIONS Official Code publication controls over unofficial compilation. - Attorneys who cite unofficial publication of 1981 Code do so at their peril; in any situation wherein defendant's compilation differs in any way from statutory provisions of the Official Code of Georgia Annotated as published by Michie (now Lexis-Nexis/srs), it is the Michie publication which is controlling. Georgia ex rel. Gen. Ass'y v. Harrison Co., 548 F. Supp. 110 (N.D. Ga. 1982), orders vacated, 559 F. Supp. 37 (N.D. Ga. 1983). Alteration of substantive law not inte
perturbation.justification: The change from Georgia law to Delaware law introduces a contradiction because it potentially undermines the legislative intent of Georgia law, as described in Georgia Code § 1-1-2 (2020). While the original text ensures the agreement is governed by Georgia law, the changed text seeks to apply Delaware law regardless of the location of the parties or confidential information, potentially conflicting with Georgia's interest in governing agreements substantially connected to Georgia. The provided snippet indicates that the enactment of the Code is not intended to alter substantive law in existence on the effective date of the Code, implying a preference for Georgia law in matters primarily related to Georgia.

perturbation.original_text: 1. C onfidential Information means any information not generally known to the public, whether recorded or conveyed in written, graphic, oral or physical form, including but not limited to scientific knowledge, know-how, processes, inventions, techniques, formulae,products, business operations, customer requirements, data, original copyrighted orcopyrightable works, plans or other records, biological materials, and/or software.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.  Confidential Information means only information explicitly marked as "Confidential" in writing at the time of disclosure.  No other information, regardless of its nature or sensitivity, will be considered confidential.
perturbation.explanation: This significantly narrows the definition of confidential information, potentially excluding information that would otherwise be protected as a trade secret under Georgia law, because trade secrets are not required to be explicitly marked as "Confidential".
perturbation.contradicted_law: Georgia Trade Secrets Act of 1990, O.C.G.A. § 10-1-761(4)
perturbation.law_explanation: O.C.G.A. § 10-1-761(4) defines "trade secret" as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. The modified definition would exclude trade secrets that meet the statutory definition but are not explicitly labeled as "Confidential," undermining the protection offered by the Georgia Trade Secrets Act.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts Georgia trade secret law. The original text broadly defines confidential information, aligning with trade secret protections. However, the changed text narrows this definition to only information explicitly marked as "Confidential," which is inconsistent with O.C.G.A. § 10-1-761(4), as explained by the LLM, and the definition of Misappropriation in the provided snippet from Georgia Code § 10-1-761 (2020). A trade secret, under Georgia law, gains its protection from its secrecy and economic value derived from that secrecy, not explicitly being marked as "Confidential."
